name: 1944 Cuba–Florida Hurricane
summary: Major hurricane of October 1944. Peak intensity 125 kt (Cat 4) over western Cuba with minimum pressure 937 mb. Crossed the Dry Tortugas as Cat 3 on Oct 18, then made mainland Florida landfall near Sarasota on Oct 19 as a strong Cat 2 (90 kt, 962 mb). Exited the NE Florida coast as a tropical storm before becoming extratropical over the Carolinas.

archives.note: First Florida landfall, Oct 18 at 9:00 PM local time; Category 3 (90 kt, 949 mb).

news.article_title: Un Ciclón Cerca de la Costa Sur de Cuba
news.article_text:
MIAMI, Oct. 14. — Un ciclón se encuentra hoy cerca de la costa sur de Cuba, según la oficina local de meteorología. Todas las precauciones necesarias deben ser tomadas y particularmente en la isla de Gran Caimán.

El meteoro está a unas 500 millas por el sur de Miami y se mueve con rumbo norte.

Estímase que la velocidad del viento aumentará en las próximas 24 horas.
news.article_text_en:
MIAMI, Oct. 14. — A cyclone is today near the southern coast of Cuba, according to the local weather bureau. All necessary precautions should be taken, particularly on the island of Grand Cayman.

The storm is about 500 miles south of Miami and is moving in a northerly direction.

It is estimated that the wind speed will increase over the next 24 hours.

Nublados. Ligeras lluvias. Vientos frescos moderados hasta el martes. Hasta la mañana de hoy no producía inquietud, en la oficina meteorológica local, el huracán del Caribe que amenaza a Cuba.
"El ciclón es muy intenso, dijo Mr. Talbott: pero se mueve con gran lentitud".
news.article_text_en:
Cloudy. Light rains. Moderate fresh winds through Tuesday. As of this morning, the Caribbean hurricane threatening Cuba was not causing concern at the local meteorological office.
"The cyclone is very intense," said Mr. Talbott, "but it is moving very slowly."

news.article_title: CREESE QUE EL CICLON PASARA ESTA NOCHE POR LA HABANA
news.article_text:
HABANA, Oct. 17. — Las últimas noticias dadas por la radio aseguran que el peligro para la Habana, por efecto del ciclón procedente del Caribe, es inminente.

El meteoro llegará a la capital cubana, si no cambia de rumbo, a las 8 de la noche de hoy.

Extraordinarias medidas se han tomado en esta ciudad, la Isla de Pinos y Pinar del Río.

Posible es que el huracán, con viento de 90 a 100 millas, azote también a la Florida.

Los cinematógrafos de la Habana no se abrirán en la noche de hoy.

En conjunto el peligro puede extenderse desde Las Villas a Pinar del Río.

El viento en la Isla de Pinos, en la mañana de hoy, fluctuaba entre 35 o 40 millas por hora.

El mayor peligro para Cuba llegará en la madrugada del miércoles.
news.article_text_en:
HAVANA, Oct. 17. — The latest news given by the radio states that the danger to Havana, from the cyclone coming out of the Caribbean, is imminent.

The storm will reach the Cuban capital, if it does not change course, at 8 o'clock tonight.

Extraordinary measures have been taken in this city, the Isle of Pines, and Pinar del Río.

It is possible that the hurricane, with winds of 90 to 100 miles, will also lash Florida.

Havana's movie theaters will not open tonight.

Overall, the danger may extend from Las Villas to Pinar del Río.

The wind on the Isle of Pines this morning was fluctuating between 35 and 40 miles per hour.

The greatest danger to Cuba will come in the early morning hours of Wednesday.

news.article_title: La Velocidad del Viento Llego Hasta 150 Millas por Hora
news.article_text:
HABANA, Oct. 18. — El huracán del Caribe, con ráfagas de 140 a 150 millas por hora, pasó por el suroeste de esta capital, a una distancia de 40 millas, entrando en el Golfo de México.

Lleva el meteoro la dirección de la Florida y estímase afectará a Miami, Fort Myers y Tampa; pero aminorada su intensidad.

En la Habana adquirieron las olas una gran altura y los vecinos del Malecón y calles cercanas buscaron refugio en lugares seguros.

El presidente Grau se mantiene en contacto con sus ministros, policía y ejército, y se manda ayuda rápida a diferentes lugares.

Sábese que han muerto dos personas en la Habana y sus alrededores. La corriente eléctrica fue cortada por disposición del gobierno, tan pronto aumentó la furia del huracán.

Ocurrieron inundaciones en algunos lugares.

A las nueve de la mañana alcanzó el ciclón su mayor intensidad.

Numerosas casas han sufrido daños de consideración.

El muelle de la Flota Blanca sufrió serios daños y después se incendió.

También ocurrió un incendio en el "Roof Garden" del Hotel Plaza.

DESAFIA UN BARCO CANADIENSE EL HURACAN

Un barco canadiense, anclado en la bahía de la Habana, levó anclas y se hizo a la mar, desafiando el huracán. Temía el capitán que se estrellase contra la costa.

Está inclinado sobre los muelles un vapor de gran tamaño.

Varias poblaciones del interior fueron evacuadas.

SE RETIRAN DEL AIRE LAS ESTACIONES DE RADIO

Las radioemisoras de la Habana dejaron de funcionar. La última en marcharse del aire lo hizo a las 8:15 de la mañana de hoy.

CUANTIOSOS DAÑOS EN BATABANO

Batabanó ha sufrido cuantiosos daños. Los alambres del telégrafo y del teléfono fueron derribados.

Muchas familias buscaron refugio en La Cabaña, Universidad y otros lugares.

Los sótanos de la Universidad fueron ofrecidos para refugio por el joven Manolo Castro, presidente del estudiantado.

En Isla de Pinos descargó el meteoro su furia.

Varios de los ministros salieron a recorrer las calles, entre ellos el de la gobernación, señor Segundo Curti.

Las radioemisoras de las provincias orientales de Cuba están funcionando.
news.article_text_en:
HAVANA, Oct. 18. — The Caribbean hurricane, with gusts of 140 to 150 miles per hour, passed to the southwest of this capital, at a distance of 40 miles, entering the Gulf of Mexico.

The storm is heading toward Florida and is expected to affect Miami, Fort Myers, and Tampa; but with diminished intensity.

In Havana the waves reached great heights, and residents of the Malecón and nearby streets sought refuge in safe places.

President Grau remains in contact with his ministers, police, and army, and rapid assistance is being sent to various locations.

It is known that two people have died in Havana and its surroundings. Electric power was cut off by government order as soon as the fury of the hurricane increased.

Flooding occurred in some areas.

At nine in the morning the cyclone reached its greatest intensity.

Numerous houses have suffered considerable damage.

The pier of the White Fleet suffered serious damage and afterward caught fire.

A fire also broke out in the "Roof Garden" of the Hotel Plaza.

CANADIAN SHIP DEFIES THE HURRICANE

A Canadian ship, anchored in Havana Bay, weighed anchor and put out to sea, defying the hurricane. The captain feared it would be dashed against the coast.

A large steamer is leaning against the piers.

Several inland towns were evacuated.

RADIO STATIONS GO OFF THE AIR

Havana's radio stations ceased operating. The last one to go off the air did so at 8:15 this morning.

HEAVY DAMAGE IN BATABANÓ

Batabanó has suffered heavy damage. Telegraph and telephone wires were knocked down.

Many families sought refuge in La Cabaña, the University, and other places.

The basements of the University were offered as shelter by the young Manolo Castro, president of the student body.

On Isla de Pinos the storm unleashed its fury.

Several of the ministers went out to tour the streets, among them the Minister of the Interior, Mr. Segundo Curti.

The radio stations of the eastern provinces of Cuba are functioning.

news.article_title: Paso El Ciclon Por Tampa En Horas De La Madrugada
news.article_text:
Noticias Contradictorias Sobre El Actual Estado Del Tiempo

Trasladadas las Tropas en Drew y McDill. Como medida de precaución fue hundido el dique flotante de la Tampa Shipbuilding.

Trasladados a hospitales especiales los Soldados. Familiares de familias de Tampa evacuadas de sus domicilios como medida de precaución, pasaron la noche de ayer en los edificios de las escuelas públicas y lugares bien protegidos.

El Ejército, con sus camiones y autos, realizó el traslado. Más de mil personas, hombres, mujeres y niños, fueron sacadas de sus casas, en los hogares cerca del astillero McCloskey, y llevadas a las escuelas Plant y otras. Más de 200 ciudadanos pasaron la noche en el Auditorio Municipal. Los soldados enfermos en la base McDill fueron llevados a un hospital establecido en el piso de uno de los hangares, todos ellos a prueba de huracán.

Los valores y documentos de valor fueron llevados a los edificios a prueba de huracán. También se tomaron, para el caso necesario, los residentes en los departamentos que el gobierno edificó en Gadsden Point. Los camiones del ejército y los autos se pusieron a disposición de los civiles.

Destrozos por el ciclón. Gran cantidad de postes y alambres del servicio eléctrico derribados. La Cruz Roja atendió a numerosas familias. Mantuvo su servicio la Compañía Telefónica Peninsular.

Mañana, hoy, el meteorólogo local dijo que el huracán del Caribe seguía al Noroeste, y los vientos del Este se sentían en el Sur de Florida, con recurvamiento hacia el norte, a 40 millas por hora.

Sobre Tampa, la mayor velocidad fue de 75 millas por hora. La Compañía Tampa Electric sufrió considerables pérdidas. Numerosos árboles arrastraron los alambres en su caída. Durante algunas horas fue suspendido el servicio de tranvías. Miles de casas empezaron a tener luz cerca del mediodía. Key West, según las noticias (Pasa a la Página 8)

LA GACETA

Sufrió Tampa la furia del ciclón del Caribe en horas de la madrugada y el viento alcanzó su mayor fuerza, 75 millas, cerca de las cinco de la mañana. Pasadas las seis, el viento se calmó y empezaron a salir los automóviles, aunque el alumbrado quedó interrumpido en varias secciones de la ciudad, abarcando a Ybor City.

La corriente eléctrica les faltó a los diarios de Ybor, "La Prensa" y LA GACETA, desde las cinco de la mañana. Ya "La Prensa" había tirado la edición correspondiente a la mañana de hoy; pero LA GACETA no tuvo sus linotipos en disposición de trabajar hasta la una de la tarde.

Numerosas noticias contradictorias circularon en la mañana de hoy, la mayor parte diseminadas por la oficina meteorológica local. A las 9 a. m. llamamos a la oficina meteorológica y se nos informó que el ciclón venía hacia Tampa. Pensamos nosotros que el meteoro había recurvado. Como a las 11 a. m. se nos dijo, a pesar de que el viento había amainado, que el ciclón se aproximaba. Más tarde, a las 12:30, cambiaron la noticia: "EL CICLON SE ALEJA. TENDREMOS LLUVIA Y VIENTOS".

Numerosos árboles han sido derribados en las calles y algunos de ellos, en Ybor City, dificultan el tránsito. Un hermoso árbol, en el (Pasa a la Página 8)
news.article_text_en:
Contradictory News About The Current State Of The Weather

Troops Relocated at Drew and McDill. As a precautionary measure, the floating dock of the Tampa Shipbuilding was sunk.

Soldiers moved to special hospitals. Family members of Tampa families evacuated from their homes as a precautionary measure spent last night in the public school buildings and other well-protected places.

The Army, with its trucks and cars, carried out the transfer. More than a thousand people, men, women and children, were taken from their homes, in the residences near the McCloskey shipyard, and taken to the Plant and other schools. More than 200 citizens spent the night in the Municipal Auditorium. The sick soldiers at McDill base were taken to a hospital set up on the floor of one of the hangars, all of them hurricane-proof.

Valuables and important documents were taken to the hurricane-proof buildings. Measures were also taken, if necessary, for the residents in the government-built developments at Gadsden Point. Army trucks and cars were placed at the disposal of civilians.

Damage from the cyclone. Great quantities of poles and wires of the electrical service were knocked down. The Red Cross attended to numerous families. The Peninsular Telephone Company maintained its service.

The local meteorologist said today that the Caribbean hurricane was continuing to the Northwest, and east winds were being felt in South Florida, with recurving toward the north, at 40 miles per hour.

Over Tampa, the maximum speed was 75 miles per hour. The Tampa Electric Company suffered considerable losses. Numerous trees dragged down wires as they fell. For several hours the streetcar service was suspended. Thousands of houses began to have light around midday. Key West, according to the news (Continued on Page 8)

LA GACETA

Tampa suffered the fury of the Caribbean cyclone in the early morning hours and the wind reached its greatest force, 75 miles, around five in the morning. After six, the wind calmed and automobiles began to come out, although the lighting remained interrupted in several sections of the city, including Ybor City.

The electrical current failed for the Ybor newspapers, "La Prensa" and LA GACETA, from five in the morning. "La Prensa" had already run off the edition corresponding to this morning; but LA GACETA did not have its linotypes in a condition to work until one in the afternoon.

Numerous contradictory reports circulated this morning, most disseminated by the local meteorological office. At 9 a.m. we called the meteorological office and were informed that the cyclone was heading toward Tampa. We thought the storm had recurved. Around 11 a.m. we were told, despite the wind having subsided, that the cyclone was approaching. Later, at 12:30, they changed the news: "THE CYCLONE IS MOVING AWAY. WE WILL HAVE RAIN AND WINDS."

news.article_title: Ha Ofrecido Su Concurso La Cruz Roja Americana
news.article_text:
Centenares de casas fueron derribadas en la Habana, Marianao, Batabanó y otros pueblos. — Limpian las calles y realizan trabajos de escombreo las brigadas de auxilio.

ESTIMASE QUE LOS PERJUICIOS SON MENORES QUE LOS QUE PRODUJERA EL METEORO DEL AÑO 1926.

HABANA, Oct. 20. — Todos los esfuerzos del pueblo cubano, con el valioso concurso de la Cruz Roja americana, se concentran hoy en la noble tarea de socorrer a los perjudicados por el pavoroso ciclón que a su paso por esta capital desarrolló una velocidad de 150 millas por hora.

El gabinete del presidente doctor Ramón Grau San Martín pasó ayer reunido varias horas y se tomaron medidas para auxiliar a los pobres y evitar que éstos pasen hambre, luego de haber perdido muchos de ellos muebles y pertenencias.

Numerosas cocinas económicas se han establecido en esta capital y su provincia, y también en las provincias de Pinar del Río y Matanzas.

El presidente Grau visitó ayer varios lugares afectados por el huracán y conferenció con los ministros de salubridad, hacienda y defensa.

La Juventud Auténtica ha realizado una admirable labor humanitaria y así se reconoce por los periódicos y las radioemisoras cubanas.

Faltan noticias de Isla de Pinos. En Batabanó los daños fueron grandes, pero menores de lo que esa población y el Surgidero sufrieron cuando el ciclón de 1926.

Telegramas recibidos de las provincias de Oriente, Camagüey y Santa Clara, informan que allí se han constituído numerosos comités de auxilio.

Han brindado su cooperación a la Cruz Roja cubana y al gobierno, sociedades humanitarias de Estados Unidos y de otros países.

Centenares de casas fueron derribadas y sufrieron enormes daños, en la Habana, numerosas industrias y casas de comercio.

DAÑOS OCASIONADOS POR EL HURACAN

Derribó el ciclón la casa del conocido periodista Arturo de Corricarte.

Varios aeroplanos del gobierno fueron destrozados y el viento tiró las torres de la Cuban Telephone Company.

Las líneas de aeroplanos comerciales experimentaron serios daños y pasarán varios días antes de que puedan reanudar el servicio.

La casa Paseo número 6, en el Vedado, se derrumbó. El tejar Andrade experimentó daños, resultando lesionado un ciudadano.

Arrasó el viento el Stadium Tropical, uno de los lugares favoritos de los deportistas cubanos.

El colegio San Eloy se derrumbó en parte, pero los niños salieron ilesos.

Centenares de edificios y casas de vivienda fueron derribados en Marianao, donde el alcalde “Panchitín” Batista realiza gestiones para que el pueblo tenga agua.

En Arroyo Arenas, cerca de Marianao, los perjuicios son considerables.

Derribó el viento varias casas en el reparto Nogueira. En Coco Solo, de los escombros de una casa, fue extraído el cuerpo de una niña polaca.

Centenares de personas se alojan en el edificio Durañona. Hay escasez de agua, velas, aceite de carbón, ropas y comestibles.

Las brigadas de auxilio limpian las calles.

A los vecinos pudientes se les ha pedido utilicen sus criados en limpiar los frentes de sus casas y de ser posible los de las casas contiguas.

Cuatro submarinos se hundieron en el muelle situado al fondo del Arsenal.

La policía marítima prestó valiosos servicios.

El secretario de Hacienda, doctor Supervielle, ha pedido un informe sobre los daños ocasionados en los muelles y en la Aduana.

Ha pedido materiales de reparación y le serán entregados, la Compañía Cubana de Electricidad.

Anoche quedó restablecido el alumbrado eléctrico en los barrios más populosos.

Los talleres del diario “Prensa Libre”, propiedad de Sergio Carbó, quedaron destruídos.

Averías de gran importancia experimentaron el edificio de la Pepsi-Cola y del ministerio de Agricultura, situado este último en la calle San Pedro. Allí se habían refugiado de 100 a 150 personas y se derrumbó una galería; pero no ocurrieron desgracias. (Pasa a la Página 8)
news.article_text_en:
Hundreds of houses were demolished in Havana, Marianao, Batabanó and other towns. — Relief brigades clean the streets and carry out debris-removal work.

IT IS ESTIMATED THAT THE DAMAGES ARE LESS THAN THOSE PRODUCED BY THE 1926 STORM.

HAVANA, Oct. 20. — All the efforts of the Cuban people, with the valuable cooperation of the American Red Cross, are concentrated today on the noble task of aiding those harmed by the terrifying cyclone which, as it passed over this capital, reached a speed of 150 miles per hour.

The cabinet of President Dr. Ramón Grau San Martín met yesterday for several hours, and measures were taken to assist the poor and prevent them from going hungry, after many of them had lost furniture and belongings.

Numerous soup kitchens have been established in this capital and its province, and also in the provinces of Pinar del Río and Matanzas.

President Grau yesterday visited several areas affected by the hurricane and conferred with the ministers of health, treasury, and defense.

The Juventud Auténtica has carried out an admirable humanitarian effort, as acknowledged by the Cuban newspapers and radio stations.

There is no news yet from Isla de Pinos. In Batabanó the damage was great, but less than what that town and Surgidero suffered during the 1926 cyclone.

Telegrams received from the provinces of Oriente, Camagüey and Santa Clara report that numerous relief committees have been formed there.

Humanitarian societies from the United States and other countries have offered their cooperation to the Cuban Red Cross and the government.

Hundreds of houses were demolished, and numerous industries and commercial establishments in Havana suffered enormous damage.

DAMAGE CAUSED BY THE HURRICANE

The cyclone tore down the home of the well-known journalist Arturo de Corricarte.

Several government airplanes were destroyed, and the wind knocked down the towers of the Cuban Telephone Company.

Commercial airline services suffered serious damage and it will be several days before they can resume operations.

The house at Paseo number 6, in Vedado, collapsed. The Andrade brickyard was damaged, and one citizen was injured.

The wind flattened Tropical Stadium, one of the favorite venues of Cuban sports fans.

The San Eloy school partly collapsed, but the children escaped unharmed.

Hundreds of buildings and dwellings were demolished in Marianao, where Mayor “Panchitín” Batista is working to secure water for the townspeople.

In Arroyo Arenas, near Marianao, the damage is considerable.

The wind tore down several houses in the Nogueira subdivision. In Coco Solo, the body of a Polish girl was pulled from the rubble of a house.

Hundreds of people are sheltering in the Durañona building. There is a shortage of water, candles, kerosene, clothing and foodstuffs.

Relief brigades are cleaning the streets.

Well-off residents have been asked to have their servants clean the fronts of their houses and, if possible, those of adjoining houses.

Four submarines sank at the pier located at the rear of the Arsenal.

The maritime police rendered valuable services.

The Secretary of the Treasury, Dr. Supervielle, has requested a report on the damage caused to the docks and to the Customs House.

The Cuban Electricity Company has requested repair materials, which will be delivered to it.

Last night, electric lighting was restored in the most populous neighborhoods.

The workshops of the newspaper “Prensa Libre,” owned by Sergio Carbó, were destroyed.

The Pepsi-Cola building and the Ministry of Agriculture building — the latter located on San Pedro Street — suffered major damage. Between 100 and 150 people had taken refuge there, and a gallery collapsed; but no casualties occurred. (Continued on Page 8)

news.article_title: Chungas y Chungas — El Dolor de Cuba / Tampa y su Generosidad
news.article_text:
El Dolor de Cuba. Tampa y su Generosidad.

EL DOLOR de Cuba, la tierra de los hombres de corazón generoso, es el dolor de Tampa, la ciudad que entregara grandes cantidades de dinero, sacadas de los bolsillos de los trabajadores, para libertar a un pueblo.

Tampa sigue con atención, pena y regocijo las noticias de Cuba.

Hace pocos días, al tomar posesión de la presidencia de la República el doctor Ramón Grau San Martín, Tampa, contentísima, aplaudió con entera sinceridad.

Había llegado una vida nueva para la isla preciosa y el tampeño no ocultaba su alegría.

Hoy, a los pocos días de haber empezado a gobernar el doctor Grau, Cuba, visitada por un horroroso ciclón, mira hacia Tampa y le comunica su pena, su profunda pena.

La "ciudad del tabaco", conmovida, emocionada, siente como suya la angustia de los cubanos y se propone, dentro de sus recursos, socorrer a Cuba.

Tampa, en diferentes etapas de su historia, con motivo de ciclones y otras desventuras, ha probado a Estados Unidos que en generosidad marcha en la vanguardia de los pueblos bondadosos de Europa y América.

Luego de dar centenares de miles de pesos para libertar a Cuba, la comunidad tampeña siguió su labor constructiva y fecunda.

Voló en auxilio de la República Española, en 1936, cuando el PUEBLO fuera salvajemente agredido por los fascistas de España, Italia, Alemania y las naciones "apaciguantes".

Todos los anhelos de justicia y superación, encuentran siempre eco en el pueblo tampeño.

Tampa se dispone ahora, sin olvidar a sus hijos, a socorrer de nuevo a Cuba.

Prestarán su valioso concurso a la obra humanitaria que es necesario desarrollar, los elementos hermanados de la colonia "latina", cubanos, españoles e italianos, y también los nobles ciudadanos de apellidos ingleses que aprecian las sólidas reacciones de "buena vecindad" existentes desde hace cincuenta años, entre el Arenal que "adoraba" Martí y la isla que cosecha el mejor tabaco del mundo.

Ayudar a quien lo necesita y no lo pide, ha sido siempre una gran satisfacción para los tampeños.

Cuba trabaja y auxilia a las víctimas. Apreciaría se la auxiliase con víveres, medicinas y ropas; pero Cuba no ruega ni implora.

Los tampeños, que conocen al pueblo cubano, se organizan para socorrer a los damnificados por el huracán y lo hacen con la mayor modestia, dulzura, cariño y noble intención.

Como en otras ocasiones, el dinero de Tampa será utilizado en beneficio de Cuba y la amistad se hará más firme y al mismo tiempo más íntima.

Los que en Cuba esperan ser socorridos, seguramente piensan: TAMPA NOS AYUDARA.

Hace algunos años, todos los elementos de la sociedad tampeña, incluyendo el alcalde, ricos ciudadanos, banqueros, profesionales, obreros de las fábricas, hicieron donativos para auxiliar a centenares o miles de cubanos infortunados.

Este periódico, y lo dice sin creer haber hecho nada extraordinario, ocupó un lugar de vanguardia en las cuestaciones hechas aquí para favorecer a los cubanos en desgracia.

Recordamos que como resultado del esfuerzo que Tampa hiciera, hace algunos años, la Cruz Roja cubana otorgó dos condecoraciones, de comendador de segunda clase, a dos ciudadanos tampeños.

Ellos nada pidieron. Una de ellas se le entregó, por haber demostrado en difíciles circunstancias el cariño que profesaba a Cuba, a Don Enrique Pendas, el fabricante de tabacos que falleciera hace algunos años.

No buscan honores los ciudadanos de Tampa y su mayor placer consiste en auxiliar a los que sufren.

Con un nuevo gobierno, al que se dejaron vacías las arcas del erario público, Tampa corresponderá espléndidamente en beneficio de Cuba y de los cubanos pobres.

Cosa sabida es que un hombre bondadoso no puede tener mayor gusto que socorrer a quien no pide, pero ha menester de apoyo o ayuda.

El gobierno cubano del doctor Grau agradecerá los donativos que espontáneamente se le manden; pero no recurrirá a la caridad exterior.

Tampa, sin embargo, es algo así como un pedazo de Cuba y quien a su FAMILIA conforta, honor merece.

Contribuya a socorrer a los cubanos damnificados por el huracán, ciudadano tampeño, y coopere así a la obra de mutuo cariño entre Cuba y Tampa, que Martí y otros cubanos iniciaron.

Jamás han negado los cubanos de Tampa su colaboración en las obras buenas de los pueblos y sociedades.

Ayer le tocó a España, la hermana y la madre, y hoy le toca a Cuba.
news.article_text_en:
The Sorrow of Cuba. Tampa and Its Generosity.

THE SORROW of Cuba, the land of generous-hearted men, is the sorrow of Tampa, the city that once delivered great sums of money, taken from the pockets of workers, to liberate a people.

Tampa follows with attention, sorrow, and joy the news from Cuba.

A few days ago, when Dr. Ramón Grau San Martín took office as president of the Republic, Tampa, overjoyed, applauded with complete sincerity.

A new life had come to the precious island, and the Tampeño did not hide his joy.

Today, only a few days after Dr. Grau began to govern, Cuba, visited by a horrific cyclone, looks toward Tampa and shares with her its sorrow, its deep sorrow.

The "Cigar City," moved and stirred, feels the anguish of the Cubans as its own and intends, within its means, to aid Cuba.

Tampa, at different stages of its history, on the occasion of cyclones and other misfortunes, has shown the United States that in generosity it marches at the vanguard of the kind-hearted peoples of Europe and America.

After giving hundreds of thousands of dollars to liberate Cuba, the Tampa community continued its constructive and fruitful work.

It flew to the aid of the Spanish Republic in 1936, when the PEOPLE were savagely attacked by the fascists of Spain, Italy, Germany, and the "appeasing" nations.

All yearnings for justice and betterment always find echo in the people of Tampa.

Tampa now prepares, without forgetting its own children, to once again aid Cuba.

Lending their valuable support to the humanitarian work that must be carried out will be the united elements of the "Latin" colony — Cubans, Spaniards, and Italians — and also the noble citizens of English surnames who appreciate the solid "good neighbor" relations that have existed for fifty years between the Arenal that Martí "adored" and the island that harvests the best tobacco in the world.

Helping those who need it and do not ask has always been a great satisfaction for the people of Tampa.

Cuba is working and aiding the victims. It would appreciate being helped with food, medicine, and clothing; but Cuba neither begs nor implores.

The people of Tampa, who know the Cuban people, are organizing to aid those affected by the hurricane, and they do so with the greatest modesty, gentleness, affection, and noble intention.

As on other occasions, Tampa's money will be used for the benefit of Cuba, and the friendship will be made firmer and at the same time more intimate.

Those in Cuba who await relief surely think: TAMPA WILL HELP US.

Some years ago, all elements of Tampa society, including the mayor, wealthy citizens, bankers, professionals, and factory workers, made donations to aid hundreds or thousands of unfortunate Cubans.

This newspaper, and it says so without believing it has done anything extraordinary, took a leading place in the fundraising drives held here to help Cubans in distress.

We recall that as a result of the effort Tampa made some years ago, the Cuban Red Cross awarded two decorations of Commander, Second Class, to two Tampa citizens.

They asked for nothing. One of them was given, for having demonstrated in difficult circumstances the affection he professed for Cuba, to Don Enrique Pendas, the cigar manufacturer who passed away some years ago.

The citizens of Tampa do not seek honors, and their greatest pleasure consists in aiding those who suffer.

With a new government to which the public coffers were left empty, Tampa will respond splendidly for the benefit of Cuba and of poor Cubans.

It is a known thing that a kind-hearted man can have no greater joy than to aid one who does not ask but is in need of support or help.

The Cuban government of Dr. Grau will be grateful for donations spontaneously sent to it, but it will not resort to foreign charity.

Tampa, however, is something like a piece of Cuba, and he who comforts his FAMILY deserves honor.

Contribute to aiding the Cubans affected by the hurricane, citizen of Tampa, and thus cooperate in the work of mutual affection between Cuba and Tampa that Martí and other Cubans began.

The Cubans of Tampa have never denied their collaboration in the good works of peoples and societies.

Yesterday it was Spain's turn, the sister and mother, and today it is Cuba's turn.

news.article_title: LA CUESTACION EN PRO DE LAS VICTIMAS DEL URACAN QUE PASARA POR CUBA
news.article_text:
Una importante reunión del Comité Pro Damnificados cubanos del huracán que pasara por Cuba y la Florida, se efectuó el sábado en la secretaría de la sociedad Círculo Cubano.

Luego de un cambio de impresiones, los reunidos acordaron nombrar varias importantes subcomisiones.

Para visitar a los fabricantes de tabacos quedó nombrada una activa subcomisión que integran los señores Guillermo Bolívar, cónsul de Cuba, Raúl Vega y Angelo Greco.

Subcomisión para solicitar donativos de los trabajadores de las fábricas: Paquito Díaz Roque, presidente del Comité Ejecutivo Conjunto; Evaristo Machín, presidente de la Unión 500, y las personas que el Comité Consultivo escoja.

Para lograr la cooperación del comercio, banqueros e industriales de Tampa: los señores Antonio Fernández, Oscar Ayala, Martín Caraballo, Raúl Aparicio, Victoriano Manteiga, Lorenzo Hernández, Johnny Díaz, José R.

(Pasa a la Página 8)

DONATIVO DE LA CRUZ ROJA AMERICANA

HABANA, Oct. 23. La Cruz Roja Americana mandó el sábado a la Cruz Roja Cubana un donativo de diez mil pesos. El dinero se destinará a socorrer a las víctimas del huracán de la semana pasada.
news.article_text_en:
FUNDRAISING DRIVE FOR THE VICTIMS OF THE HURRICANE THAT PASSED OVER CUBA

An important meeting of the Committee for Cuban Victims of the hurricane that passed over Cuba and Florida was held Saturday at the secretariat of the Círculo Cubano society.

After an exchange of views, those gathered agreed to appoint several important subcommittees.

To visit the cigar manufacturers, an active subcommittee was appointed, composed of Mr. Guillermo Bolívar, consul of Cuba, Raúl Vega, and Angelo Greco.

Subcommittee to solicit donations from the workers of the factories: Paquito Díaz Roque, president of the Joint Executive Committee; Evaristo Machín, president of Unión 500, and such persons as the Consultative Committee may choose.

To secure the cooperation of the commerce, bankers and industrialists of Tampa: Messrs. Antonio Fernández, Oscar Ayala, Martín Caraballo, Raúl Aparicio, Victoriano Manteiga, Lorenzo Hernández, Johnny Díaz, José R.

(Continued on Page 8)

DONATION FROM THE AMERICAN RED CROSS

HAVANA, Oct. 23. The American Red Cross sent on Saturday to the Cuban Red Cross a donation of ten thousand dollars. The money will be used to aid the victims of last week's hurricane.

Grau se propone d
news.article_text:
CONTRA LOS EXPLOTADORES del pueblo, ha empezado a luchar en Cuba, con admirable energía, el gobierno del doctor Ramón Grau San Martín.

El sábado fueron encarcelados varios "bodegueros" por vender las papas a un alto precio.

LA ORPA, que equivale en Cuba a la OPA, ha dispuesto que los dueños de establecimientos de víveres pongan sus listas de precios a la vista del público, de modo que sólo puedan cobrarse los precios justos y legales.

¡Quien se niegue a fijar las listas en los lugares indicados, será detenido y castigado!

Con motivo del huracán, algunos comerciantes elevaron los precios a su gusto, criminalmente, y ahora deberán rendir cuentas por sus errores.

Grau se propone darle a Cuba una VIDA NUEVA, de base cívica, y ya lo viene logrando.

Allá, en la isla primorosa, las mercancías no podrán ser escondidas, como se hace en otros lugares.

Ayudar al presidente Grau y su gobierno es contribuir al progreso de un país que anhela cumplir los postulados de los ciudadanos que por su independencia combatieron.

[...]

CIUDADANO: Mande su donativo al Comité Pro Cuba, constituído en la sociedad Círculo Cubano.

Varias subcomisiones empezarán a trabajar inmediatamente y se acercarán a obreros, industriales, comerciantes, profesionales y a todos los ciudadanos de buena voluntad y corazón generoso.

En Cuba, el huracán que alcanzara una velocidad máxima de 160 millas por hora, ocasionó daños valuados en muchos millones de dólares.

Batista dejó vacías las cajas de la hacienda cubana y debemos acudir en socorro de los damnificados, haciendo así menos difícil la obra de reorganización iniciada por el nuevo gobierno.

En la sociedad cubana de la calle 14 será bien recibido su donativo.
news.article_text_en:
AGAINST THE EXPLOITERS of the people, the government of Dr. Ramón Grau San Martín has begun to fight in Cuba with admirable energy.

On Saturday several "bodegueros" (grocers) were jailed for selling potatoes at a high price.

The ORPA, which is Cuba's equivalent of the OPA, has ordered that the owners of food establishments post their price lists in plain view of the public, so that only fair and legal prices can be charged.

Anyone who refuses to post the lists in the indicated places will be detained and punished!

On the occasion of the hurricane, some merchants raised prices as they pleased, criminally, and now must answer for their wrongdoing.

Grau proposes to give Cuba a NEW LIFE, on a civic foundation, and he is already achieving it.

There, on the lovely island, merchandise cannot be hidden, as is done in other places.

Helping President Grau and his government is contributing to the progress of a country that longs to fulfill the ideals of the citizens who fought for its independence.

[...]

CITIZEN: Send your donation to the Pro-Cuba Committee, formed at the Círculo Cubano society.

Several subcommittees will begin work immediately and will approach workers, industrialists, merchants, professionals, and all citizens of good will and generous heart.

In Cuba, the hurricane, which reached a maximum speed of 160 miles per hour, caused damage valued at many millions of dollars.

Batista left the coffers of the Cuban treasury empty, and we must come to the aid of the victims, thus making less difficult the work of reorganization begun by the new government.

Your donation will be gladly received at the Cuban society on 14th Street.

Una recomendación del alcalde Hixon, de modo que la campaña contra los mosquitos sea intensificada, alcanzó anoche el apoyo de los señores concejales.

Aprobaron los ediles una resolución elogiando el trabajo del doctor Pease, jefe de salubridad del condado y el doctor Parran, secretario de salubridad en el gabinete del presidente Roosevelt.

Varios informes fueron leídos, entre ellos uno del señor Sanborn, superintendente de parques, sobre los árboles derribados por el huracán y la limpieza de arbustos y yerbas.

A Johnny Olmedo se le concedió un permiso para vender bebidas alcohólicas en el número 1512 de calle 16.
news.article_text_en:
CAMPAIGN AGAINST MOSQUITOES

A recommendation by Mayor Hixon that the campaign against mosquitoes be intensified received last night the support of the city councilmen.

The aldermen approved a resolution praising the work of Dr. Pease, head of county health, and Dr. Parran, Secretary of Health in President Roosevelt's cabinet.

Several reports were read, among them one by Mr. Sanborn, superintendent of parks, on the trees knocked down by the hurricane and the clearing of shrubs and weeds.

Johnny Olmedo was granted a permit to sell alcoholic beverages at 1512 16th Street.

news.article_title: Storm to Hit Jax In Late Afternoon
news.article_text:
Reliable information this morning is that the mighty tropic storm which blew out of the gulf and brought considerable damage to Havana, Cuba, and the Miami section yesterday struck the St. Petersburg and Tampa area this morning and was turning to the northeast. It was expected to reach Jacksonville sometime in the late afternoon if it continued on its present course.

Reports are that the hurricane is still about 300 miles wide which means that all of North Florida east of the Apalachicola will feel the gale to varying degrees.

In the Tampa area schools and shipyards had been evacuated and every precaution taken against the loss of life and property. In its center the hurricane was said to have reached a velocity of 160 miles per hour. It is traveling forward at about 12 miles per hour.

news.title: The St. Augustine Record — 1944: A dangerous year
news.summary: According to the source, the Cuba-Florida Hurricane struck St. Augustine on October 19-20, 1944, just days after a major downtown fire, arriving as a Category 2 storm after weakening from Category 4 over Cuba. The article states the hurricane brought 100 mph winds and a 15-foot storm surge, flooding parts of the city up to 5 feet deep, destroying the St. Augustine Beach pier, and collapsing the Bridge of Lions. It reports the storm killed 23 people in Florida and caused $50 million in damage, making it the second major hurricane to hit the state that year.

news.title: Brooksville Journal — citrus growers biggest sufferers from tropical hurricane
news.snippet: "Hernando county citrus growers will be the biggest sufferers from the tropical hurricane which visited the state and this section last night and this morning at an early hour... damage, however, to citrus can be considered slight in comparison with other sections where the storm proved to be the worst."
news.source: The Brooksville Journal — LOC Chronicling America
news.article_title: HERNANDO CITRUS GROUP HARDEST HIT BY STORM THAT VISITS THE COUNTY
news.article_text:
Growers Believe Grapefruit Crop Will Suffer Most

Hernando county citrus growers will be the biggest sufferers from the tropical hurricane which visited the state and this section last night and this morning at an early hour, according to estimates made by leading business men and others here. The damage, however, to citrus can be considered slight in comparison with other sections where the storm proved to be the worst. The center of the disturbance seemed to have been south of Brooksville, between Fort Myers and Tampa, coming in from the Gulf and heading across the state towards Jacksonville and Cape Hatteras, off the coast of North Carolina. The storm started in the Carribean Sea, passing Cuba and then taking a direct northward path.

The damage to citrus in this county will be less than 25 percent as estimated by the Brooksville Citrus Growers Assn. Grapefruit will show the greatest loss, officials have stated. The entire loss to citrus growers in the state will run around $20,000,000.

In this county, high tides were recorded at Aripeka and Bay Port but little damage was done along the coast. Many of the county roads leading to Brooksville were blocked by falling trees and washout. School buses were unable to operate. All schools were closed for the day, but will commence again Friday morning at the usual hour.

In Brooksville many trees were blown down and the streets and yards of residents cluttered with moss, fallen limbs and other debris. Some business houses lost their signs and roofs were affected. The roof of the Weeks Hardware Company was damaged to some extent and the metal roof of the McKeown Motor Company torn off. A southbound Florida Motor Line bus was stopped here during the early morning hours and the occupants taken to the county jail where they remained until the storm subsided. They were treated to breakfast and hot coffee by Sheriff Neil F. Law, his deputies and officers connected with Florida State Highway Patrol. Only two buses passed the city during the day headed for Tampa and other points south. No northbound buses from Tampa were recorded during the entire day.

The negro section of Brooksville seemed to have received the worst effect of the storm. Many roofs of houses were blown off and a house owned by B. G. White, negro minister, upset from its blocks. During the night more than 35 negro refugees were housed in the Brooksville colored school building, the work of caring for them being done under the direction of W. D. Hicks, principal.

news.title: Brooksville Journal — vegetable losses 25-90%, OPA asked to remove ceilings
news.snippet: "On the basis of Florida vegetable losses ranging from 25 to 90 percent due to the hurricane, the Florida Vegetable Committee has requested that all vegetable price ceilings be removed by the Office of Price Administration..." Also: power-company line crews were brought in from as far as Atlanta and Birmingham.
news.source: The Brooksville Journal — LOC Chronicling America
news.article_title: VEGETABLE LOSS IN STATE RUNS TO LARGE FIGURE
news.article_text:
On the basis of Florida vegetable losses ranging from 25 to 90 percent due to the hurricane, the Florida Vegetable Committee has requested that all vegetable price ceilings be removed by the Office of Price Administration on account of the disaster, it was announced by LaMonte Graw, committee manager at Orlando.

Particular emphasis was placed on urgent insistence that ceilings on cucumbers and snap beans for October be removed immediately. Chairman Loring Raoul met with FVC committee officials in Sarasota Tuesday to plan strategy.

Former FVC Chairman E. W. (Pete) Lins of S. Miami, Florida, member of the national Over-all Fresh Vegetable Industry Advisory Committee, immediately filed a protest on vegetable ceilings with OPA, War Food Administration and other interested agencies at Washington, citing the severe damage to beans, tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, and eggplant ranging from 25 to 90 percent.

Manager Graw, at the Orlando headquarters of FVC, is correlating loss statistics, tabulated from preliminary reports over the state.

"These reports are not final," Graw stated, "But they give us an indication of conditions until the official survey of the Bureau of Agricultural Economics, on which OPA bases its decisions, is made available. The BAE crop reporters began their official survey on Sunday.

"However, on the basis of preliminary reports from the industry in Florida, it is indicated that out of 35,000 to 40,000 acres of snap beans which were planted, from 60 to 90 percent are damaged. Harvesting of snap beans had already started.

"The cucumber crop, on which harvesting also had been started, will suffer a loss of from 70 to 90 percent on a total of 7,000 acres planted.

"Approximately 6500 acres of tomatoes had been planted and our preliminary estimates place the loss between 70 and 90 percent.

"Out of 1500 acres of peppers and 1200 acres of eggplant that had been planted, damage to the extent of 60 to 80 percent is reported.

"There is heavy loss to the potato crops in the Ft. Myers and Lake Okeechobee areas, and unestimated damage to the celery crops at Sarasota, Sanford and Belle Glade. Lesser losses were suffered in other vegetable commodities."
